title: Portfolio evidence and interviews
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to constructing a compelling CAS portfolio and successfully navigating the three formal interviews. It focuses on curating meaningful evidence, writing analytical reflections, and articulating personal growth in line with the seven CAS learning outcomes.
learningObjectives: Understand the purpose of the CAS portfolio as a developmental tool for showcasing growth.
Curate and select diverse and meaningful evidence that demonstrates engagement and achievement.
Articulate personal development through analytical reflections that connect experiences to the seven learning outcomes.
Prepare effectively for the three formal CAS interviews to discuss progress, challenges, and overall achievements.
Distinguish between descriptive accounts and meaningful reflections that demonstrate self-awareness.

simpleExplanation.title: Your CAS Story: Crafting a Powerful Portfolio
simpleExplanation.summary: The CAS portfolio is not just a scrapbook of activities; it is the curated story of your personal journey through Creativity, Activity, and Service. It's where you collect evidence and reflect on your experiences to show how you have grown and met the seven key learning outcomes.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of your CAS portfolio as a film director's 'behind-the-scenes' documentary. The final film is your completed CAS programme, but the portfolio shows the storyboards (planning), the on-set challenges (reflections on difficulties), the interviews with actors (your reflections on collaboration), and the director's notes (your analysis of personal growth). It proves not just what you did, but what you learned and how you developed in the process.
steps: Plan & Propose: Begin by outlining your interests and goals for CAS, using the portfolio to document your initial plans for experiences and your CAS project.
Collect & Curate: As you undertake experiences, gather varied evidence (photos, documents, videos, testimonials) that authentically represents your engagement and challenges.
Reflect & Connect: Regularly write reflections that go beyond description. Analyse your experiences, connecting them explicitly to the seven learning outcomes to demonstrate your development.

Review & Articulate: Use your curated portfolio as the basis for your three formal interviews, preparing to discuss your journey, growth, and achievement of the learning outcomes with your CAS coordinator.

flashcards.back: A collection of evidence and reflections that documents a student's CAS journey. It showcases planning, engagement, and achievement of the seven learning outcomes.
Any item that demonstrates participation or learning in a CAS experience. Examples include photos, videos, planning documents, emails, certificates, or creative works. Quality and relevance are key.
A written or verbal analysis of a CAS experience. It should explore thoughts, feelings, challenges, and personal growth, explicitly connecting the experience to one or more learning outcomes.

Description states what happened ('We painted a mural'). Meaningful reflection analyses the experience ('Organising the team required me to develop new leadership skills, especially in delegating tasks, which I initially found difficult...').
The core goals of CAS. The portfolio and interviews must demonstrate how the student has achieved all seven outcomes through their experiences.

Demonstrating the ability to stick with experiences over time, showing dedication and resilience in the face of challenges. Evidence could be a log of attendance or a reflection on overcoming a setback.
Demonstrating the ability to work effectively with others. Evidence could include meeting minutes, a group photo with a reflective caption, or a testimonial from a teammate.
Occurs at the beginning of the DP. Focuses on discussing the student's interests, plans for CAS, and understanding of the programme's requirements and the seven learning outcomes.
Typically occurs around the middle of the DP. A progress review to discuss experiences undertaken, challenges faced, and progress towards meeting the learning outcomes.
Occurs at the end of the DP. A summative discussion where the student uses their portfolio to provide a final account of their CAS journey and demonstrate how they have fulfilled all requirements.

A collaborative, sustained project of at least one month's duration that challenges students to show initiative, perseverance, and develop collaborative skills. It must involve at least one of the C, A, or S strands.
A key principle of CAS. Students are expected to take initiative in planning, organising, and reflecting on their experiences, with guidance from their CAS coordinator and advisors.

faq.q: How much evidence do I need for my portfolio?
faq.a: Focus on quality over quantity. You do not need evidence for every single hour. Instead, choose representative evidence for your key experiences that best illustrates your engagement and learning. A few powerful, well-chosen pieces with strong reflections are far more effective than a huge folder of uncurated photos.

faq.q: What if I don't have a photo or video for an experience?
faq.a: Visuals are not the only form of evidence. You can use journal entries, planning documents, emails confirming your participation, a written description of a skill you learned, or a testimonial from a supervisor. The key is that the evidence is authentic and supports your reflection.

faq.q: What is the difference between a CAS reflection and a TOK reflection?
faq.a: A CAS reflection is personal and focuses on your development, feelings, challenges, and growth in relation to an experience (e.g., 'How did this change my perspective?'). A TOK reflection is analytical and focuses on knowledge questions that arise from a situation (e.g., 'How can we know if this action produced a net positive impact?'). CAS is about you; TOK is about knowledge itself.

faq.q: Can I fail my CAS interview?
faq.a: The interviews are not pass/fail tests but supportive dialogues. Their purpose is to guide you. However, failing to attend them or being completely unprepared and unable to discuss your progress could demonstrate a lack of engagement with the CAS process, which could jeopardise your successful completion of CAS. Treat them as a mandatory and important part of the programme.

sections.content: The CAS portfolio is the central component of your CAS journey. It is not merely a final submission for assessment, but a dynamic, ongoing record of your growth over the two years of the Diploma Programme. This lesson will guide you in transforming your portfolio from a simple logbook into a powerful narrative that effectively demonstrates your engagement, development, and achievement of the seven learning outcomes, preparing you for successful formal interviews.
The Purpose of the CAS Portfolio

The IB defines the CAS portfolio as 'a collection of evidence that showcases CAS experiences and student reflections on those experiences'. Its primary purpose is to provide a space for you to document your journey and demonstrate to your CAS coordinator, and yourself, that you have met the requirements of the programme. A high-quality portfolio is not judged on its bulk, but on the quality and thoughtfulness of its contents. It should be a source of pride and a clear representation of your development.

items: It is a developmental tool, not just an assessment task. It helps you plan, reflect, and recognise your own growth.
It provides the structure for your three formal interviews with your CAS coordinator.
It must demonstrate engagement in Creativity, Activity, and Service experiences, including a CAS Project.
Crucially, it must contain clear evidence and reflections showing how you have achieved each of the seven learning outcomes.

sections.content: Evidence is what substantiates your claims of participation and learning. The best evidence is authentic, varied, and directly linked to your reflections. Move beyond simply uploading a single photo. Consider what combination of items best tells the story of your experience, from planning to execution to reflection. Your evidence should make your engagement tangible to someone who wasn't there.

items: **Planning Documents:** Proposals, email exchanges with organisations, budget spreadsheets, risk assessments, meeting agendas.

**Process Documentation:** Photos or short videos of you engaged in the activity (not just posing!), screenshots of collaborative online work (e.g., a shared Google Doc), audio recordings of music practice.
**Final Products:** A copy of the pamphlet you designed, a link to the website you built, the final artwork, a recording of your performance.
**Feedback & Recognition:** Certificates of participation, a thank-you letter from an organisation, a written testimonial from a supervisor or teammate (with their permission).

sections.question: For your CAS Project, you and a team organised a sports tournament for younger students to raise awareness about healthy lifestyles. Select and justify three distinct pieces of evidence that together demonstrate Learning Outcome 5: 'Demonstrate the skills and recognise the benefits of working collaboratively'.
sections.solution:
To demonstrate successful collaboration, I would select evidence that shows the process, not just the final event.

**Evidence 1: A screenshot of our team's shared planning document (e.g., Trello board or Google Sheet) with assigned tasks and deadlines.**
*Justification:* This piece of evidence directly demonstrates the practical skills of collaboration. It shows how we divided a complex project into manageable tasks, assigned roles based on individual strengths, and held each other accountable. It is tangible proof of our organisational and collaborative process, moving beyond a simple claim that we 'worked together'.

**Evidence 2: A photograph of a team meeting where we are actively debating the schedule, with a reflective caption.**
*Justification:* While a photo alone is descriptive, adding a caption transforms it into powerful evidence. My caption would read: 'This photo captures a moment of intense debate about the tournament schedule. Initially, we had conflicting ideas, but through active listening and compromise, we reached a consensus that worked for everyone. This experience taught me that collaboration isn't just about agreeing, but about navigating disagreements constructively to achieve a shared goal.' This directly addresses the 'recognise the benefits' part of the learning outcome.

**Evidence 3: A short, written testimonial from a teammate.**
*Justification:* External feedback provides an objective perspective on my collaborative skills. I would ask a teammate to comment on my role in the group, for example: '[Student's Name] was crucial in keeping our meetings on track and ensuring everyone's voice was heard. When we faced a challenge with booking the venue, they facilitated a brainstorming session that led to our solution.' This provides specific, third-party validation of the collaborative skills I aimed to develop.

sections.content: The Art of Reflection: Moving from Description to Analysis

Reflection is the heart of CAS. It is where learning is articulated and demonstrated. Many students fall into the trap of simply describing what they did. To excel, you must move into analysis, exploring the 'so what?' of your experience. Use the Gibbs' Reflective Cycle (Description -> Feelings -> Evaluation -> Analysis -> Conclusion -> Action Plan) as a mental model. Your reflections are your primary method for explicitly showing how you have met the seven learning outcomes.

sections.question: You joined a beginners' coding club (Creativity) and found it very difficult at first. Write a reflection of approximately 200 words that addresses Learning Outcome 1: 'Identify own strengths and develop areas for growth'.
sections.solution:
Initially, joining the coding club was a significant challenge. My strength has always been in humanities subjects, and I identified my logical problem-solving skills as a definite area for growth. The first few weeks were frustrating; I struggled to grasp basic Python syntax while others seemed to progress quickly. I felt discouraged and questioned my ability to contribute.

Rather than quitting, I decided to address this weakness directly. I began spending extra time after school working through online tutorials and asked a more experienced club member to mentor me. This was a step outside my comfort zone, as I am not used to asking for help. The turning point was when I finally debugged a small programme on my own. The feeling of accomplishment was immense. 

This experience has shown me that my perceived 'weakness' in logical thinking is not fixed. By applying perseverance—another skill I developed (LO4)—I was able to tangibly improve. I now understand that growth often happens in areas of initial difficulty, and I am more confident in tackling unfamiliar technical challenges in the future, both in and out of academics.

sections.content: When writing reflections, use the language of the learning outcomes directly. Start sentences with phrases like, 'In developing my collaborative skills (LO5), I learned that...' or 'This challenge forced me to show perseverance (LO4) when...'. This makes it easy for your CAS coordinator to see you are consciously addressing the requirements.

The three formal interviews are not examinations but structured, supportive conversations about your CAS journey. They are mandatory checkpoints that help you and your coordinator ensure you are on track. Use your portfolio as your script and evidence base for these meetings.

items: **First Interview (Beginning of DP1):** The 'Planning' interview. Come prepared to discuss your interests, initial ideas for experiences and the CAS project, and how you plan to address the seven learning outcomes. Show you understand the goals of CAS.

**Second Interview (End of DP1/Start of DP2):** The 'Progress' interview. Review your portfolio beforehand. Be ready to discuss the experiences you've completed, the challenges you've faced, and your progress towards the learning outcomes. This is a good time to adjust plans if needed.

**Final Interview (End of DP2):** The 'Summative' interview. This is your final presentation. You will use your completed portfolio to narrate your entire CAS journey, providing a compelling case, with evidence and reflection, that you have successfully achieved all seven learning outcomes and completed all programme requirements.

sections.content: For your final interview, don't just show your coordinator your portfolio; guide them through it. Prepare a few key 'exhibits' – a piece of evidence and reflection for each learning outcome – that you can talk about in detail. This demonstrates ownership and deep understanding of your own growth.
